name: Grammar: Sentence Types and Transformations

sourceRefs.label: civilserviceaspirants.in - 2 dedicated TNPSC MCQ sets (Exclamatory-into-Statement, 6 questions; Imperatives-into-Interrogatives, 6 questions)
2025_q1 page 20 Q86-87 & 38; page 40 Q90-92; page 41 Q93-94 (real, with answer key marks)

researchNotes: Fifth Grammar topic — exceptionally dense evidence (18+ real hits found, narrowed to the 11 most distinct transformation types to avoid padding with near-duplicate exclamatory drills). One real exam item (Q93) was itself flagged in the source paper as 'to be placed before the expert committee for final decision' — i.e., TNPSC's OWN answer key had unresolved doubt about it. Included anyway since it's realistic exam evidence, but flagged transparently rather than presented as a clean fact.

reading.intro: Covers converting between the 4 sentence types (statement/declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory) and related transformations (assertive↔negative-equivalent, assertive↔imperative).

qa.question: Real exam: convert the interrogative 'Is she not changing her dress?' into a statement.
qa.answer: She is not changing her dress. (A negative interrogative converts to a plain negative statement, keeping the same tense — don't flip it to a positive statement.)

qa.question: Real exam: transform 'He is too shrewd to be cheated' into a negative-equivalent sentence.
qa.answer: He is so shrewd that he cannot be cheated. ('too...to' restructures to 'so...that...cannot', preserving the original meaning exactly — not weakening it to 'not too shrewd'.)

qa.question: Real exam: convert the exclamatory 'Had she known it earlier!' into an assertive sentence.
qa.answer: I wish she had known it earlier. (This exclamatory form — an inverted conditional expressing regret — converts to an assertive 'I wish...' statement, not a plain factual statement like 'She knew it earlier'.)

qa.question: Real exam: which of these is an imperative sentence — 'Who are you?' / 'How did you explain this question?' / 'Clean your room' / 'What a great statue!'?
qa.answer: 'Clean your room' — a command/request, with no stated subject (the subject 'you' is implied).

qa.question: Real exam: 'Let my head be shattered to pieces' is what type of sentence?
qa.answer: Imperative sentence — 'Let...' constructions expressing a wish/command (despite sounding dramatic/exclamatory) are classified as imperative in this exam's pattern.

qa.question: Real exam: transform the assertive 'You should answer all the questions' into an imperative sentence.
qa.answer: Answer all the questions. (Drop the subject 'you' and the modal 'should', keep just the base-form verb command.)

qa.question: Real exam: transform the imperative 'Give me a book' into an appreciative statement.
qa.answer: I appreciate you giving a book. (An 'appreciative statement' transformation expresses gratitude/appreciation for the action, distinct from a plain 'Thank you for...' — both can sound plausible, so match the exact phrasing style asked for.)

qa.question: Real exam (flagged by TNPSC itself as sent to its expert committee for final decision — included with that caveat): the interrogative form of 'You have to be there on Monday' was marked as:
qa.answer: Will you be there on Monday? (NOTE: this exact item's answer was itself flagged as disputed/under review in the source answer key — a reminder that even official answer keys sometimes have contested items; don't treat every 'marked' answer as infallible.)

qa.question: Convert into a statement: 'What a beautiful painting!'
qa.answer: It is a beautiful painting. ('What a/an + adjective + noun!' converts to 'It is a/an + adjective + noun.')

qa.question: Convert into a statement: 'How sweet the mango is!'
qa.answer: The mango is very sweet. ('How + adjective + subject + is!' converts to '[Subject] is very + adjective.' — note the added 'very', which is the standard pattern for this conversion.)

qa.question: Convert the imperative 'Please sit down' into an interrogative.
qa.answer: Can you sit down? (Polite imperative → 'Can/Will you...?' request-question form.)

qa.question: Convert the imperative 'Be quiet' into an interrogative.
qa.answer: Will you be quiet? (Direct imperative → 'Will you...?' form — note this differs slightly from the 'Can you...?' pattern used for 'Please sit down', so match the exact style of the original imperative.)

questions.prompt: Convert into a statement: 'Is she not changing her dress?'
options: She will not change her dress.
She was not changing her dress.
She is not changing her dress.
questions.explanation: She is not changing her dress.

questions.prompt: Transform into a negative-equivalent sentence: 'He is too shrewd to be cheated.'
options: He is not shrewd enough to be cheated.
He is so shrewd that he cannot be cheated.
He is not too shrewd to be cheated.
questions.explanation: He is so shrewd that he cannot be cheated.

questions.prompt: Convert the exclamatory sentence 'Had she known it earlier!' into an assertive sentence.
options: I wish she had known it earlier.
She should have known it earlier.
questions.explanation: I wish she had known it earlier.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an imperative sentence?
options: How did you explain this question?

questions.prompt: 'Let my head be shattered to pieces' is an:
Transform into an imperative sentence: 'You should answer all the questions.'
options: Should you answer all the questions?

questions.prompt: Transform the imperative 'Give me a book' into an appreciative statement.
options: I'm glad that you have given a book.
Thank you for giving a book.
I appreciate you giving a book.
Why do you not give a book?
questions.explanation: I appreciate you giving a book.

questions.prompt: Convert into a statement: 'What a beautiful painting!'
Convert into a statement: 'How sweet the mango is!'
Convert the imperative 'Please sit down' into an interrogative.
Convert the imperative 'Be quiet' into an interrogative.
